I mean the football analogy is it's the red zone. So all the statistics in the middle of the field are nice, but if you can't get in the end zone and all you can do is kick field goals, you're still going to get beat. Right, exactly. So Nick, one of the things that you talk about and you tell your team at GKIC and other people who you've trained is that everyone, every entrepreneur at their core needs to be a sales person. Talk about that a little bit. Yeah, I couldn't agree. As the business owner we talked about, Dan brought up, it's your job to bring the money in. And many times that money is the sales, closing the sales for the organization. I think they get caught up in that or it's messy or they don't want to do that. And many of them haven't been trained or aren't good and they get in the way. We find that a lot also. And really everybody is a sales person to the person answering the phone, to the waitress in the restaurant. She's a sales person. We had an experience last night where she wasn't a good sales person, right? A good sales person will build a check average. We were actually afraid of her. We were afraid of her. We wanted to get out as fast as we could. But you know, a good, well, they'll build the check average. They'll ask for the sale. They'll assume the sale. They'll bring the desserts to the table and ask people to pick. So really everybody is at the heart of it as a sales person. Let's go to the extreme. Dan brought up football but I'll go to Hollywood, right? So if we turn the TV on right about now, we'd see the view. We'd see Ellen. We'd see all the... Those are your favorite shows. Absolutely. Love Ellen. This is actually what you're doing when you close the shade at the GKIC headquarters. I watched the view after we were being bid because of all the blood spurting out of it. But go ahead. No, no, no, I agree. So you know, there's somebody from Hollywood selling their movie, right? They're selling. There's a musical person selling their album or their concert. There's an author selling his book. We're all selling. The HBO movie, The Entourage. I love the part where he said, they paid me $2 million to make this movie. And his agent said, no, they paid you $2 million to sell the movie. Your job is to go out there and sell the movie. And I think we're all... I'll give you both ends of the extreme. So this morning I'm at Panera Bread, the breakfast, because we don't... They bring in lunch but we don't... So I overhear two of the staff members talking. And so one says to the other, I worked at McDonald's before this. I'm making more money here. But at McDonald's we were asked to upsell. Here we're expected to upsell and I don't really like it. So of course she needs to be removed from the cash register and handed a broom. And she's only worth minimum wage, not $15 an hour, not $50 an hour. Nobody gets that, but that's the reality. So at every level you see to your point, you see people that not only don't know how to do it, don't understand it, they have disdain and distaste for it. It's like, ew, that's dirty. Well, they've got to be removed from everywhere unless they are a person in a room who has no interaction whatsoever with the public. So if you've got, like in a dental practice, if you've got somebody who does the insurance billing and as long as you don't let her answer the phone, leave the room in the back, see a patient even walking past them, that person's got to leave by the back door and not the front door. Then it doesn't matter if she hates sales. But otherwise you have big problems all the way to the top. So I thought about it about a month ago. So somebody asked me this, a good question. Because I dabble as a stock market investor, is there any commonality in your losses? Interesting. Good question. Really good question. So here's the commonality. Every single time I violate my own litmus test, which is that the CEO or a very top dog ought to have hardcore direct sales experience. They ought to have some time if sold belly to belly, nose to nose, toes to toes in the trenches and they ought to still have enthusiasm for it. Every single time I've violated that, I've lost money. Wow. You know, why do you think it is though, like we see this a lot with the professionals, with the doctors, with the lawyers, that they think somehow this is below them. Why do you think that is and what is the fix for them? Hopefully it's watching this and taking notes and saying what we're going to do, but what is the psychology there? You know, I find it difficult because you've spent all this money to open up the practice, right? You've invested all this money to be the best at your profession and you won't do the things you need to do to put the money in the pocket of the business owner, which is yourself.
